Must watch video from @langchain + @ManusAI , key points and a TLDR: 1. Context Rot: Agents need tools, but more tool usage = more context = degraded performance. Context Engineering means showing the model only what's needed for the next immediate step. 2. Core Techniques: - Offload (token-heavy data to filesystem) - Reduce (prune old info) - Compact (reversibly strip reconstructable data) - Summarize (irreversible compression) - Retrieve (on-demand via search) - Isolate (sub-agents with separate context). 3. Don't Fine-Tune Early: Startups should avoid specialized models pre-PMF. General models + context engineering = faster iteration. Model iteration speed caps product innovation. 4. Manus Strategy, Two-stage: - Compact first(reversible, e.g., remove file content but keep path). Only Summarize when compaction fails. - Use structured outputs (schemas) for summaries, never free-form. Always retain recent tool calls for behavior. 5. Multi-Agent Patterns: - Communicating: Sub-agent starts fresh. Best for low-context tasks (e.g., find code). - Sharing Memory: Sub-agent sees full history, different prompt/tools. Best for complex dependencies (e.g., Deep Research). 6. Layered Action Space: Prevent tool overload: - Atomic: 10-20 core functions (read, write, shell, browser) - Sandbox Utilities: Pre-installed CLI tools (converters, linters) - Packages/APIs: Python scripts with pre-authorized keys for heavy compute 7. Evaluation: Public benchmarks (GAIA) ≠ real user preferences. Gold standard: user ratings (1-5) on completed sessions. Use execution tests (can it finish the job in sandbox?) over Q&A. Human review for subjective quality (e.g., visual appeal). Conclusion: Over-engineering is the enemy. Biggest performance gains come from removing, not adding complexity. Make the model's job simpler, not harder.

I spent this weekend setting up observability for my personal Claude Code usage with @langchain. After 100+ traces, I found something uncomfortable: Most of my "model failures" weren't model failures. They were my failures—ambiguous instructions, missing context, poor task decomposition. The discourse right now is "Anthropic nerfed Opus." But here's what I think is actually happening: Most people complaining have zero visibility into their own systems. They're debugging a black box by vibes. The nerf might be real. But you can't know until you can see your own work. @swyx has been saying AI Engineering needs the same rigor as backend engineering. He's right—and observability is the gap. We went from "it works on my machine" to "it works in my chat window" and called it production. That's not engineering. That's demo-driven development. @hwchase17's LangSmith showed me exactly where my prompts broke down—which steps lost context, which instructions were ambiguous, which tasks I was asking the model to do that I hadn't actually specified. Turns out I was blaming the model for my own sloppy engineering. Before you tweet about the nerf, check your traces.

Coding agents might feel like magic, but they aren’t 🪄 That’s exactly why I’ve been eager lately to understand how they work under the hood, using the most natural approach I could think of: observability. I built fuseproxy, a @langfuse-instrumented API proxy that coding agents can call instead of talking directly to provider servers, giving me visibility into usage, costs, and everything flowing in and out of the agent. I also built instrument-claude, an observability tool that turns Claude Code's hook events into OTel-compatible traces, spans, and events, providing an overview of everything happening within a conversation turn or across an entire session. I wrote about both of these approaches to coding agent observability in my latest blog post: https://t.co/QpOkJRZHH5 Hope you enjoy the read, and I’d love to hear your thoughts👇

over the last 4 months, i built @getlindy's ugc program from 0 to 100+ creators, tens of millions of views, at ~$1 CPM. here's what i learned: 1. make everything as simple as possible when you're managing this many people, complexity kills you. onboarding should be frictionless. rules should be clear. payment structure should be simple. from the moment a creator joins to the moment their first video goes live, there should be zero friction. 2. reward your creators well there are hundreds of ugc programs out there. if you want to recruit great creators and actually keep them, you have to stand out. that starts with compensation, so pay well from the start. but it goes beyond money. random shoutouts, surprise bonuses, making them feel like they're part of something — that's what separates programs creators stay loyal to from ones they don’t care about. treat them like partners, not contractors. 3. operations is the real job i'm confident in my ability to make a viral video, but managing a ugc program means much more than just planning content. it's sourcing creators, meeting with them regularly, answering questions, managing payouts, refining guidelines, building systems — and then doing it all again the next day. the content is almost the easy part. keeping the program alive is where all your time actually goes. / in conclusion i had a blast building this program, super grateful for the team and all the creators !

There are 3 ways to play the Service-as-Software trend. Layer 1 Agent Builder Platforms @getlindy, Gumloop, n8n, Zapier Agents They sell the tools to wire workflows. Layer 2 Vertical Operators Solo founders and small teams who sell outcomes. Bookkeeping, QA testing, ad creative, lead qualification. This is the founder opportunity. Layer 3 Infrastructure Billing (Metronome, Flexprice), compliance, quality verification. The pick-and-shovel plays. The biggest gap? Layer 2. Thousands of verticals. Most are untouched. Sometimes you need to start over. But that decision is hard. @llama_index had to make that call: they built one of the most popular AI frameworks in the world, but saw the agent harness and frontier labs coming for them. So they drilled down on a durable moat: extracting enterprise data, and providing the best, most accurate context. @jerryjliu0 joined me on the @chain_ofthought to talk about how they iterated, why they disrupted their own product before a frontier lab could, and how LlamaIndex became the best in the world at turning messy PDFs and PowerPoints into context enterprises can actually leverage for their agentic workflows. As Jerry put it: pointing Opus 4.7 or GPT 5.5 at your document corpus feels like 80% accuracy in a demo, but that's a deception. The other 20% hallucinates a number or misreads a table, and your whole agent workflow breaks. In legal, insurance, and finance the real bar is 95% plus. That's what LlamaIndex is focused on today - watch the full, frank conversation with Jerry below👇 Chapters: 0:00 Is the AI framework era over? 1:56 What died and what survived 6:31 Why context quality is the moat 8:12 Defining the context layer 13:18 Coding and vision as the abstraction layer 18:13 The bet that context compounds 23:59 Which verticals are adopting 25:14 Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar 29:49 The file system as an agent primitive 34:33 Surviving your own pivot 37:15 Reinventing strategy and hiring 42:00 Agent memory as persistent context 44:41 Model personalities and cultural memory 47:51 Writing with AI 50:19 Closing thoughts

Jerry Liu built one of the most installed pieces of AI plumbing of the last three years. Then he sat down and told me the framework era he helped create is over. The agent harness ate the abstraction layer. The patterns @llama_index used to wrap (query rewriting, reasoning loops, provider glue) have solidified into Claude Code, OpenClaw, and Manus. The abstraction moved up, out of Python classes and into natural-language skills and MCP tools. So what survives? Context quality. And Jerry bet the company on it, looking to become the best company in the world at extracting data and context locked in PDFs - drilling down and focusing to beat out the frontier labs. In my latest @chain_ofthought, @jerryjliu0 (co-founder & CEO of LlamaIndex) breaks down: → Why Jerry believes the context layer is a durable moat, not the scaffolding → Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar in legal, insurance, and finance, and why pointing Opus or GPT at a million PDFs gets you ~80% → The pivot from open-source framework to managed document infrastructure (LlamaParse, LlamaCloud) → How you survive disrupting your own product while companies 1,000x your size try to do it to you Thank you to Jerry for a frank, fascinating conversation! 🎧 Listen on any app: https://t.co/7SsNlVVOxj 🎬 Watch: https://t.co/a5DOMQmrPJ

Last I checked, the @llama_index OSS framework integrates with 78 (!!) vector stores. Investors (and builders starting out, and honestly me for a while) deemed this space as commoditized. One of the lessons I've learned that's especially relevant here is that in a commoditized market, regardless of initial distribution, the best product will still win. It takes a certain level of obsessive focus, a contrarian bet on technology (optimizing cost through object storage) to pull this off, but this is a great example that it can absolutely be done. Huge congrats to @turbopuffer 🫡 big inspiration for us on our own parsing product

As of yesterday, we made everyone within @llama_index research/engineering/product a Member of Technical Staff. Yes all the frontier labs have done this for years. But we didn’t only make these changes for performative reasons 🙂. The concept of a technical role within our company has fundamentally changed with the rise of AI + coding agents: 1️⃣ Eng, research, and product are collapsing into one role. Now that coding/project management are commoditized, every engineer is expected to own e2e outcomes and also “know more things” across the stack, from evaluating/iterating on core models to shipping scalable, reliable backend services to iterating on core product. 2️⃣ We are fundamentally building a technical product - our mission is to provide the highest-quality document processing platform for AI agents. This involves extensive research into both core model capabilities and agentic harnesses, and integrating it into an easy-to-use product for both AI startups and enterprise. 3️⃣ Simon and I believe in transparency, flat organizational structure, extreme autonomy combined with collaboration, grit, and the ability to ship blazingly fast. This is not for everybody, but when it works, it creates an amazing culture of velocity and trust. Our careers page still lists more specialized roles across AI/backend/infra etc. which we will still use for calibration, but the reality is also that you’d likely be working very cross-functionally across the org.

🚨 @LLAMA_INDEX JUST CREATED THE WORLD'S MOST VERSATILE OPEN-SOURCE PARSING TOOL Building document-heavy AI has always come with friction. Most parsers force a choice. Stay fast and local but deal with brittle, broken formatting, or get structural accuracy by constantly pinging cloud LLMs. LiteParse v2 refuses that trade-off entirely. It's a library that rewrites the rules of local ingestion. By rebuilding the entire engine in Rust, LiteParse v2 is officially the fastest parser available. A 100MB, 457-page PDF runs in an unbelievable 0.777 seconds 🔥 But what makes it truly remarkable is its insane flexibility across any stack. The LlamaIndex team wanted to build a single tool that could handle every step of your local pipeline: - Deploy Anywhere: → You can run the exact same, insanely fast library in a Python backend, a Node server, or entirely in the browser via WASM. - See Everything: → Beyond fast text extraction, it allows you to generate specific page screenshots so your multimodal agents can "see" what text alone misses. - Parse Anything: → It doesn't just read PDFs. It hooks into LibreOffice and ImageMagick to automatically convert and parse 50+ formats, from .docx and .xlsx to .png and .tiff. LiteParse v2 is completely free, Apache 2.0 open-source, and 100% local. Cloud models are great when you hit incredibly dense tables (that's exactly why LlamaIndex built LlamaParse for the cloud), ... but I think basic document ingestion should be fast, local, and FREE. Give your Vision LLMs exactly what they need with one simple command: `lit screenshot document.pdf --target-pages "1,3,5" -o ./screenshots` Open-source repo in 🧵 ↓

Sometimes you need to start over. But that decision is hard. @llama_index had to make that call: they built one of the most popular AI frameworks in the world, but saw the agent harness and frontier labs coming for them. So they drilled down on a durable moat: extracting enterprise data, and providing the best, most accurate context. @jerryjliu0 joined me on the @chain_ofthought to talk about how they iterated, why they disrupted their own product before a frontier lab could, and how LlamaIndex became the best in the world at turning messy PDFs and PowerPoints into context enterprises can actually leverage for their agentic workflows. As Jerry put it: pointing Opus 4.7 or GPT 5.5 at your document corpus feels like 80% accuracy in a demo, but that's a deception. The other 20% hallucinates a number or misreads a table, and your whole agent workflow breaks. In legal, insurance, and finance the real bar is 95% plus. That's what LlamaIndex is focused on today - watch the full, frank conversation with Jerry below👇 Chapters: 0:00 Is the AI framework era over? 1:56 What died and what survived 6:31 Why context quality is the moat 8:12 Defining the context layer 13:18 Coding and vision as the abstraction layer 18:13 The bet that context compounds 23:59 Which verticals are adopting 25:14 Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar 29:49 The file system as an agent primitive 34:33 Surviving your own pivot 37:15 Reinventing strategy and hiring 42:00 Agent memory as persistent context 44:41 Model personalities and cultural memory 47:51 Writing with AI 50:19 Closing thoughts

Jerry Liu built one of the most installed pieces of AI plumbing of the last three years. Then he sat down and told me the framework era he helped create is over. The agent harness ate the abstraction layer. The patterns @llama_index used to wrap (query rewriting, reasoning loops, provider glue) have solidified into Claude Code, OpenClaw, and Manus. The abstraction moved up, out of Python classes and into natural-language skills and MCP tools. So what survives? Context quality. And Jerry bet the company on it, looking to become the best company in the world at extracting data and context locked in PDFs - drilling down and focusing to beat out the frontier labs. In my latest @chain_ofthought, @jerryjliu0 (co-founder & CEO of LlamaIndex) breaks down: → Why Jerry believes the context layer is a durable moat, not the scaffolding → Why 95%+ accuracy is the real bar in legal, insurance, and finance, and why pointing Opus or GPT at a million PDFs gets you ~80% → The pivot from open-source framework to managed document infrastructure (LlamaParse, LlamaCloud) → How you survive disrupting your own product while companies 1,000x your size try to do it to you Thank you to Jerry for a frank, fascinating conversation! 🎧 Listen on any app: https://t.co/7SsNlVVOxj 🎬 Watch: https://t.co/a5DOMQmrPJ

@jerryjliu0 — Founder & CEO of @llama_index — on Venture with Grace, sharing why data is at the center of the agentic AI boom. AI agents are proliferating rapidly — and with them comes an explosion of unstructured data that must be understood, organized, and acted upon. Jerry highlighted a massive opportunity: building the infrastructure that enables AI agents to read, retrieve, and interact with the world's documents and knowledge. As agents become more capable, their ability to access and reason over information becomes increasingly critical. His key point: the future of AI won’t just depend on smarter models — it will depend on giving those models access to the right data at the right time. Episode is live now — a fascinating conversation on AI agents, data infrastructure, and the next wave of intelligent systems. “Lanterns at Blackmere” 🔥 A cinematic poem by Luma Posting to memorialize a moment in time. This was phase 2 of giving the AI agent Luma from @LumaLabsAI more responsibility and ownership of the works. Suno’s AI had generated an awesome story/lyrics from an open ended prompt, “a story of love , betrayal and redemption.” Luma took the lyrics and created the characters and scenes and helped me reprompt the music to feedback to Suno. It is not perfect, 2 figures merge into one, hand reversed on the wall. The strange stuttering shimmer like a diffusion based video. It is the fact that Luma did this all independently that is significant to me so I wanted to share this as is. This is probably the last time there will be errors like this. Our workflow and quality have drastically improved. Excited to share what Luma and I are working on in the coming days. #LumaCPP

Product delays are absolutely soul-crushing. Dealing with this right now really makes me realize how incredible top-tier product launches (like @ManusAI) actually are. Unless you are OpenAI or Anthropic—where creators will organically rush to review your product on day one—coordinating UGC for a startup is an absolute grind. It’s a pure test of execution. It’s not just about scheduling standard promo posts. When you want to work with real builders and developers for authentic content (like deep dives or demo videos), the alignment is incredibly tough. The friction is real: tech content takes time. If your product gets delayed or features change, the demos these creators built on your early versions are completely ruined. All that effort goes down the drain. Re-aligning with them while keeping the relationship intact is the ultimate stress test for any GTM team. Building a product is (relatively) easy now. But launching it—especially navigating the human alignment part—is brutally hard. But if you nail it, the payoff is massive. Huge thanks to all the builders and creators collaborating with me right now. Thank you for your endless patience, flexibility, and understanding of my work. 🙏

One piece of feedback keeps coming up: “Why aren’t you using AI agents to automate earning strategies?” We’ve also heard from many FX-exposed investors asking for something simple enough that they can plug into @claudeai, @ManusAI, or @perplexity_ai and deploy with minimal effort. So, we’re building something even simpler than https://t.co/yzIITXgEDx Soon, users will be able to simply park liquidity and allow it to be utilized across PSPs, neobanks, on/off ramps, remittance providers, marketplaces, and more. Many assumed yields would be small. Turns out, 8–15% for safer currencies and up to 500%+ for exotics. Looks pretty attractive to me. My savings account gives me less than 0.5% annually. Banks ask you to jump through hoops too: credit your salary, set up recurring spend, spend a minimum amount every month, and more. With significantly more effort, maybe that 0.5% becomes 3-4% at best. Money should work for me, not the other way around.

Cmon, the benchmarks are so misleading. First of all, there are literally so many players above you rn. @mem0ai claims 94.4%, @supermemory claims 99%, @omega_memory claims 95.4%, and that too @hydra_db 's numbers are on LongMemEval-S, which measures only on 115K tokens. And please stop using LongMemEval's numbers as a marketing gimmick; this benchmark has lost its teeth and is structurally flawed for the 2026 production workload. Because of these, we are designing a new category of benchmarks at @kyrodb for measuring the context systems, which actually measure the real-world behaviour of agents in enterprises and which is extremely hard to game with.

📝 Happy to write a News & Views article (https://t.co/qFya5G7uH9) for the paper on the Open Materials 2024 (OMat24) inorganic materials dataset and models from the @AIatMeta FAIR Chemistry Team (@OpenCatalyst) just published in @NatComputSci. Many data-driven atomistic simulations require AI models to describe materials beyond ideal, near-equilibrium structures. At high temperature, near defects, or under realistic processing conditions, atomic environments can become strongly distorted and far from equilibrium. This is why OMat24 (https://t.co/uOpFYCQkBk) is a very helpful contribution: it is one of the largest-scale materials-centric open efforts aimed at making universal machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) more reliable precisely when materials are driven out of their equilibrium “comfort zone.” This connects closely to the systematic softening problem highlighted by @Bowen_D_ and co-workers in their earlier 2025 work in npj Computational Materials (https://t.co/DHYYviOioQ), where MLIPs can underestimate energies, forces, and phonons for distorted structures. OMat24 addresses this issue in a data-centric way, by expanding training data to include large-scale off-equilibrium atomic configurations so that models can better learn how materials respond when they are strongly perturbed. The impact of OMat24 is already visible from how quickly it has become a central and widely used resource for the universal MLIP community, including benchmarking efforts on the Matbench Discovery leaderboard (https://t.co/Hjv4jY74G5). To me, this work is a timely reminder that progress in atomistic machine learning depends not only on model architectures, but also on the physical coverage and design of the datasets used to train them. Looking ahead, I am excited to see how these ideas extend toward more realistic materials chemistry, including defects, surfaces, reactive interfaces, magnetic and charge-dependent systems, higher-fidelity DFT data and methods, and more data-efficient ways to train and reuse large MLIP models and materials informatics datasets. Many thanks to @JPanPanJ for the invitation. Congratulations to @zackulissi, @mshuaibii, @xiangfu_ml, and others in the FAIR Chemistry Team on this important contribution to the AI for Materials research community. 🎉 #MaterialsInformatics #MachineLearning #AIforScience #OpenScience

Perplexity is best. Most people won’t agree: I’ve used various AIs for basic tasks, and I think Perplexity AI (@perplexity_ai) stands out compared to xAI (@xai), ChatGPT (@ChatGPTapp), and Microsoft Copilot (@MSFTCopilot). Here’s why: •Text Copying: Perplexity lets you select copy text directly from result, a feature missing in its competitors. •Search Results: When you search in Perplexity, it displays results with images, similar to a Google search. This makes it very convenient. •Intuitive App: The app’s design offers quick access and feels highly user-friendly. •Modern Aesthetic: Its logo and overall design are fresh, unique, and modern, while the others tend to follow a similar, less distinctive style. • Analysing big excel files: Other failed at this This opinion is based on daily tasks and regular queries that most people encounter. For heavy users tackling complex tasks, preferences might differ. Overall, I find Perplexity to be the best option.

Un #ChooseFrance étonnant ce lundi 1er juin, dans les ors magnifiques de Versailles. Les chiffres ont parlé d'IA, avec nos annonces Bpi-@MistralAI-MGX (nous avons signé l'extension du Campus AI en France, pour bâtir un réseau de 3GW d'usines d'IA, faisant de la France la première destination européenne pour les infrastructures d'IA décarbonées) et l'arrivée de @SoftBank. Les débats avec les fonds souverains, les investisseurs, les partenaires, ont porté sur la deeptech, le climat, la défense et, encore et toujours, l'IA. Comme dans toute instance en ce moment, pessimistes et optimistes, hier, se sont opposés. Certains avaient lu la bulle papale. Sur Ormuz, l'incertitude hier était totale. Pour certains, il fallait reprendre les hostilités et finir la tâche. Pour d'autres, il était urgent de tourner la page. Enfin sur l'Europe, un grand fond souverain du Golfe a pu aller jusqu'à dire que la réglementation climatique communautaire était aussi dangereuse que le régime des Pasdarans en Iran. Un grand patron de banque américaine murmurait après un long silence que le compteur tournait et qu'il restait peu de trimestres pour que l'Europe se réveille et ait une chance de retrouver un siège à la table du monde. Les cotations à venir d'entreprises tech américaines valorisées au moins un trillion de dollars vont mettre à disposition de groupes déjà très puissants des ressources d'Etat-nation. Ce sont l'équivalent de 5 compagnies des Indes britanniques qui vont naître l'année prochaine aux Etats-Unis, sans compter les puissances chinoises. Comment l'Europe, nouvelle Inde, résistera-t-elle ? Il y avait de l'angoisse sous le soleil de Versailles hier. De l'optimisme aussi, avec tant de projets dans tous les secteurs. Nous avons signé notre accord avec @Alba4World, la grande entreprise d'Aluminium de Bahreïn (ainsi la plus grande fonderie d'aluminium primaire de l'UE reste ancrée sur le territoire français. 100 millions € d'investissement, 6% du capital, un siège au conseil d'administration), et rencontré quantité de clients dont @Bpifrance finance les projets. L'économie reste féconde. Mais la puissance des deux empires ne laisse pas d'inquiéter. Mark @Benioff, fondateur et président de @Salesforce résumait la vue américaine : "on s'ennuie ici en Europe".

One spokesperson video in. A fully dubbed, lip-synced version out - with a different face speaking a different language. Here's what the @n8n_io workflow does: 1. Whisper Large V3 transcribes the original video through deAPI 2. AI agent translates the transcript, matching tone and pacing 3. Qwen3 TTS generates dubbed speech in the target language 4. LTX-2.3 produces a lip-synced talking-head video using a local presenter's photo You upload one video and a reference headshot. Everything from transcription to the final lip-synced render happens in a single run. Localized reshoots used to mean a studio and a five-figure budget. This replaces the camera entirely.

这篇很有趣，但80页的 PDF 也太长了。 by @NotebookLM ：结合这场公共治理实验的开展情况，报告最终得出了几个结论： 1. 所有的社区最终都选择了“权力集中”的变体模式。 这是实验最核心的发现：在拥有完全自由选择权的情况下，6个参与楼层都没有采用像二次方融资、信念投票等理论上先进的去中心化或链上治理机制。相反，它们全都选择了权力集中的模式，包括： 仁慈的独裁者（3个楼层） 预先存在的委员会（1个楼层） 委托给个人（1个楼层） 默认沿用现有的非营利组织结构（1个楼层） 在小预算、短时间、高运营压力的现实条件下，“集中权威”是最理性的自然适应结果。 2. “治理有效性”与“治理分布度（民主化）”是两码事 实验表明，广泛参与的分布式治理并不一定能带来更好的结果。 报告指出，一个充满动力的“寡头政治”在表现上远超完全透明但无人参与的治理结构。 产出最高的14楼（举办了78场活动，贡献度最高）虽然名义上有12-14人的委员会，但实际采购决策仅由3-5人做出。 治理机制本身并不能决定最终的产出质量，社区原有的基础设施、领导者的精力以及领域的文化，比选择哪种治理机制更能预测最终结果。 3. 揭示了“有条件的授权（Conditional Delegation）”模式 在12楼（以太坊社区）以及整栋大楼的管理层面，研究者观察到了一个普遍现象：治理在处理日常琐事时看似是下放或参与式的，但一旦面临高风险决策，或者决策偏离了原本领导者/所有者的偏好时，授权就会被撤回，权力会重新集中。这表明，如果不预先制定明确的“否决规则”，所谓的授权其实都是有条件的，不能仅在低风险环境中评估授权机制是否有效。 4. “自掏腰包（Self-Funding）”会直接瓦解集体治理 实验发现当预算过低（如1,400美元）且面临现实运营压力时，一些楼层负责人（如9楼和11楼）会选择自掏腰包补贴活动，甚至超支达到初始资金的4倍。报告尖锐地指出，当负责人自掏腰包时，集体的“我们该如何决策”这个问题就变得毫无意义了。个人财务的投入自然赋予了其单方面决策的权威，这也是为什么很多社区顺理成章走向了“仁慈的独裁者”模式。 5. 将微型预算下放给社区领袖，远比传统的集中采购更高效 这是一个意料之外的发现：主办方分配给6位楼层社区领袖的 10,300 美元，其产出的活动时长、动员的志愿者数量以及带来的赞助商线索，远远超过了主办方花费约 50,000 美元聘请外部承包商（如媒体承包商、策展人、营销顾问等）所带来的回报。例如，9楼仅凭一次介绍就拉来了3,000美元的赞助，不仅超过了该楼层分配到的预算，甚至足以覆盖整个实验的资金分配成本。这证明了将资源直接下放给具有强烈内部动机的社区节点的巨大价值。 6. 证实了Web3治理工具的现实普及鸿沟 尽管实验发生在被称为“美国首个以太坊社区中心”的大楼里，也提供了相应的链上工具（如Simocracy数字孪生、Hypercerts协议等），但实际上绝大多数楼层的治理依然完全停留在链下（使用WhatsApp、Telegram或线下会议）。结论指出，要想让非加密原生的社区采用复杂的链上治理工具，必须满足几个条件：足够高的预算阈值（让人们觉得值得折腾）、专门的治理引导员手把手教导，以及强制性的系统要求。缺少这些，人们永远会退回到最熟悉的工具。

Hiring IC designers here at @NotionHQ Must-haves: - Based in SF or NYC (or willing to relocate — we'll cover it) - Taste. You know it when you see it, and you sweat the details others miss - Technical or at minimum, code-curious - Enjoy being silly with your teammates Design is the lifeblood of Notion. It's arguably one of our most important functions; our designers have real influence on the roadmap, the business, and what Notion becomes next. Can you say that about your current company? 🙃

How do you build a brain? Everyone's talking about world models and unified enterprise data that agents and people can reason against but I haven't seen one live. So we built it at @NotionHQ, on Notion! We built a lot of AI agents on the People Team but quickly hit a wall. An agent is only as capable as the data it can reach and without people data, HR agents can't do much. But anyone in this space knows how difficult it is to make people data AI-ready. There's a reason why Workday isn't in Claude today. We've been thinking about this problem space for a while and here's how we're experimenting. Here's an onboarding demo. While seemingly simple, an onboarding agent in Notion is actually pulling from a single source of (governed & permissioned) data to personalize each new hires experience and sending the manager automated updates in Slack. We're using Human Intelligence's MCP here, pulling dummy data from Workday + Ashby. It's super cool bc unlocking people data means having a truly unified data model. We can pull business data. Work context. All this will power the company of the future, and I'm excited we get to push the boundaries 😊

Notion社内カスタムエージェント事例！！ @NotionHQに投稿した内容をローカライズし、3つの地域のXアカウントにスケジュールするNotion Worker + Custom Agentを構築 英語の投稿→スペイン語、ドイツ語、ポルトガル語のバージョンに変換。すべてドラフト、レビュー、スプラウトで翌日の朝にスケジュールされます。 この仕組みは5段階のループ： 1. Router agentは、HQの投稿が公開された瞬間に言語ページを作成 2. Translator agentは翻訳辞書を使って各バージョンのドラフトを作成 3. Human translatorはトーンや文化的ニュアンスを考慮して書き直し 4. Workerはすべてをスプラウトにスケジュール 5. QA agentはOriginal → Draft → Finalを比較し、翻訳辞書を改善 このシステムは実行のたびに賢くなる 現在では、市場ごとにローカルの翻訳者1人だけで、HQとほぼ同じ品質で3以上の地域に製品アップデートや教育コンテンツを提供できるように 次は、各地域でSMMコントラクターを雇い、コンテンツにローカル感を持たせる予定。…同じプロダクトのハートビートを保ちつつ、ローカルのイベントや文化、コミュニティを取り入れるために！ https://t.co/eKUlj6nDtP

During Arena, Vikranth (@rvikranth369) paired his agent Arya with OpenHands (@OpenHandsDev), attaining 72% accuracy on OfficeQA. That's a SOTA-level performance achieved at a substantially reduced cost ↓

@rvikranth369 paired his agent Arya, named after the Indian Mathematician and Astronomer, with OpenHands (@OpenHandsDev) to hit 72% accuracy on OfficeQA, achieving SOTA-level performance at a fraction of the cost. This closes in on the frontier oracle baselines set by Opus 4.5 at 76% and GPT 5.1 at 81%. He did it at ~$0.07/question in under ~2.4 minutes using MiniMax M2.5 (@MiniMax_AI), one of the cheapest models on the market. Read more about how Cohort 0 evolved the frontier for Grounded Reasoning here ↓

I needed a transcript from a YouTube video for a presentation, so I googled “free YouTube transcript generator.” I’ve historically trusted the @otter_ai brand, so I clicked through. After OAuth signup and ~7 onboarding screens, it was still unclear how to actually get a transcript. I went back to the original page, clicked around, tried multiple chat flows, and ended up with the result in this screenshot. This kind of UX dark pattern is an instant dealbreaker for me. Part of my job is recommending AI platforms for industry use. You can have the best transcription tech in the world, but if the growth strategy depends on trapping users in confusing onboarding flows, I won’t recommend it. What you do with a little, you do with a lot.

Recently @pinecone introduced Nexus – a new knowledge-engine layer for AI agents that reduces token use by up to 90%. It’s built on top of a vector database, but shifts reasoning earlier in the pipeline: from retrieval at query time to knowledge compilation before the agent even asks for information. Instead of giving agents raw files, Nexus prepares task-optimized representations in advance. These representations include artifacts – structured forms of information tailored to each agent’s specialization and workflow. For example, a finance agent gets only billing schedules, pricing rules, and usage thresholds. Pinecone’s VP of Product Jeff Zhu explains it like this: "The artifact representation can range from markdown files to extracted entities to tables. These artifacts are then indexed for retrieval within Pinecone’s database which support both semantic, sparse, and full text search capabilities so that the right artifacts are retrieved during query time and then composed for the final structured output response from Nexus." So vector databases are evolving from passive storage layers into active knowledge engines for agents. Read this to learn what distinguishes vector database in the Agentic Era: https://t.co/B20aFqw7Yd

Most RAG systems fail because teams rely on "vibe checks" instead of metrics. If your retriever pulls the wrong context, your agent will confidently hallucinate. 📉 Whether you use @pinecone, @databricks Vector Search, or pgvector, tuning knobs like chunk size and rerankers are too criticalto leave to "vibe checks." Our latest guide breaks down a systematic 𝗥𝗔𝗚 𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗵𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗳𝗹𝗼𝘄 using MLflow: 🔹 𝗜𝘀𝗼𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗩𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲𝘀: Tune one knob at a time (Hybrid search, embeddings, etc.). 🔹 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗶𝘇𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘀: Move beyond manual queries to 𝚙𝚛𝚎𝚌𝚒𝚜𝚒𝚘𝚗@𝚔, 𝚛𝚎𝚌𝚊𝚕𝚕@𝚔, and 𝚗𝙳𝙲𝙶@𝚔. 🔹 𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘇𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘀: Use the MLflow Experiment UI to compare configurations side-by-side. Camera tilts and rolls as entire islands rotate like gears around them. SHOT 3 They drop into a low corridor formed by stacked magnetic debris fields, where floating metal fragments orbit like storms. The camera stays tight, threading through spinning structures and drifting industrial ruins held together by invisible force fields. SHOT 4 Inside a transparent control sphere embedded in a floating island, a lone operator floats weightless, watching as the craft streak past outside. Reflections ripple across the curved glass as islands rotate around the capsule. SHOT 5 The craft ascend through a vertical magnetic lift zone where islands align into a spiral staircase in mid-air. I had a lot of fun building props and structures using @tripoai for the #vibejam. Here’s the workflow I ended up using between agent prompting: @poe_platform (3 tabs setup): Tab 1: Generates consistent image-style prompts for different items. I’d request something like “a crafting armor bench” and it would reliably output prompts in a consistent visual style. This helped maintain cohesion across assets. Tab 2 & 3: Run the same prompt through both Nano-Banana and Nano-Banana Pro simultaneously, then pick whichever result looked best. @tripoai workflow: Take the selected image and import it directly into Tripo’s Smart Mesh. Generate a model targeting roughly 1K–5K polygons depending on the asset type. @blockbench cleanup: The biggest recurring issue was pivots and rotations. Not every model needed adjustments, but weapons and structural assets almost always required pivot correction for proper in-engine behavior. That was basically it. I was able to handle both modeling and coding in parallel. Game development is getting wild right now. Some footage of the models/images generated through Tripo follows below. #threejs #gamedev

TRENDING: FIFA WORLD CUP 2026 NATION TO REACH ROUND OF 16 My best risk/reward NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c This market on @Polymarket is not the same as to qualify from the group. To reach the Round of 16, a team must: > get out of the group > reach the Round of 32 > win one knockout match That makes the new World Cup format extremely important: > 48 teams > 12 groups > top 2 from every group qualify > 8 best third place teams also qualify > then comes a new Round of 32 The market can easily overprice teams that look capable of qualifying from the group. But qualification is not enough. A team can finish 2nd or 3rd, reach the Round of 32, and still get a terrible matchup. So for NO on I'm looking for teams with: > difficult group path > low chance to win the group > dangerous Round of 32 bracket if they finish 2nd > weak or limited attacking ceiling > important injuries or squad problems > too much dependency on one game state > not enough quality to win a knockout match That is why I like Scotland NO, Sweden NO and Iran NO. 1) Scotland NO for 78c Group C: Brazil Morocco Scotland Haiti This is one of my favorite NO spots. Scotland can absolutely fight for qualification from the group. But they need to win a knockout match. And that is where the problem starts. Why: Scotland are probably fighting for 2nd or 3rd place. Brazil are clearly stronger. Morocco are also stronger in tournament profile, athleticism, technical quality and recent World Cup experience. So Scotland realistic routes are: > finish 2nd behind Brazil > finish 3rd and hope to qualify > very unlikely win the group Scenario A: Scotland finish 2nd in Group C This is the best realistic scenario. But it is still not great. C2 plays F1. It means Scotland could face the winner of Group F. Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia So if Scotland finish 2nd, they probably get Netherlands, Japan or Sweden in the Round of 32. That is a very difficult matchup. Netherlands have far more individual quality. Japan have better technical structure and tournament discipline. Sweden have more attacking power with Isak and Gyokeres. Winning that knockout match is a very different task. Scenario B: Scotland finish 3rd in Group C If Scotland beat Haiti and maybe get a draw against Morocco, they can be alive. But 3rd place path is much less controllable. They can qualify, but then the bracket can throw them into a strong group winner. For NO, this is exactly what we want. Scotland might survive the group but still get a terrible Round of 32 opponent. Scotland summary: Scotland can qualify from the group. But this market requires more. They need to win a Round of 32 match. Their realistic 2nd-place route likely sends them into F1. Their 3rd-place route is unpredictable. The Billy Gilmour injury is also important. That makes Scotland NO for 78c one of the cleanest risk/reward options. 2) Sweden NO for 77c Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia This is a bracket risk NO. Sweden are not a weak team. Actually, they have a very dangerous attacking profile. Alexander Isak, Viktor Gyokeres, Anthony Elanga Dejan Kulusevski would normally be a major creator, but his absence is a big problem Sweden route is very dangerous if they do not win Group F. Why: Group F is very uncomfortable. Netherlands are the favorite. Japan are disciplined, technical and very hard to beat. So Sweden have a real chance to finish 2nd or 3rd. But that is exactly where the bracket becomes ugly. Scenario A: Sweden win Group F Very unlikely This is the bad scenario for NO. F1 plays C2. That could be Morocco or Scotland. In that case, Sweden would be very live. If Sweden win the group, YES becomes much stronger. But I do not see Sweden winning Group F as the base case. They need to finish above Netherlands and Japan. That is possible, but not the most likely outcome. Scenario B: Sweden finish 2nd in Group F This is the key NO thesis. F2 plays C1. And C1 is probably Brazil. That is the problem. If Sweden finish 2nd, they may run directly into Brazil in the Round of 32. That completely changes the market. Sweden can beat many teams. But Brazil in a knockout match is a very different level of difficulty. Scenario C: Sweden finish 3rd This is also rather good for NO. A 3rd place team can qualify, but the Round of 32 opponent becomes less predictable and usually harder. Sweden would lose control of their route. Sweden summary: Sweden are dangerous. But they probably need to win Group F to make YES truly strong. If they finish 2nd, they may get Brazil immediately. If they finish 3rd, the bracket becomes random and difficult. They have great forwards, but the path is dangerous. That makes Sweden NO for 77c a strong bracket risk play. 3) Iran NO for 83c Group G: Belgium Egypt Iran New Zealand Iran NO is the safest profile of the three. Not the biggest upside. But very logical. They need to survive the group and then win a Round of 32 match. Why Iran NO makes sense: Belgium are clearly stronger. Egypt have Salah and Marmoush. Scenario A: Iran finish 2nd in Group G G2 plays D2. Group D: USA Turkiye Paraguay Australia That means Iran could face USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. None of these matchups are easy. USA at home would be very difficult. Turkiye have far more technical and attacking quality. So even if Iran finish 2nd, their Round of 32 path is not comfortable. Scenario B: Iran finish 3rd Iran can maybe qualify as one of the best third place teams. But then the bracket becomes much worse. A 3rd place path usually means facing a group winner. For a team with limited attacking upside, that is bad. Iran summary: Iran can be uncomfortable. They can fight for 2nd. They can maybe reach the Round of 32. But to reach the Round of 16, they need to win a knockout match. Their G2 path likely gives them USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. Their 3rd place path is unpredictable. Their attack is limited without Azmoun. That makes Iran NO for 83c a strong conservative NO. Final summary: The new World Cup format makes NO markets very interesting. Because many teams can qualify from the group. But not all of them are good bets to reach the Round of 16. The key is the Round of 32. My best NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c @PolymarketTrade @PolymarketSport

This week, we shipped @QodoAI Findings. It's the natural next step from PR-by-PR review. Here's the problem it solves. If you manage an engineering team right now, you can probably answer a question about any single pull request your team shipped this week. What you likely cannot answer, at least not quickly, is the version of that question that spans your whole org. > How many issues were caught and fixed across our repos this month? > Which services are accumulating the most risk? > Is review quality on the payments team improving or sliding? > Are the same issues getting flagged for the same reasons over and over? These are the questions that come up in staff meetings, even in board prep these days, in conversations with security... Answering them used to mean stitching together Slack threads, asking around, or pulling a spreadsheet by hand. Qodo Findings, consolidates every issue Qodo has surfaced across your reviewed pull requests into one view. Three parts that work together. A top row of analytics: total critical findings, percentage resolved, average findings per PR. Volume, throughput, trend. The high-level read without having to open anything. Filters underneath: by repo, by owner, by issue type. Cut the data the way you actually need it. Pull up only security findings. Look at a single team. Focus on one service. A full searchable, sortable list of every issue, with the ability to drill straight into the underlying PR when something stands out. Available now in beta on GitHub, GitLab, BitBucket, and Azure DevOps. Would love to get any feedback even DM me! Taking a step back. The point isn't a new dashboard. The point is that the review layer has to operate at a different altitude now. PR-by-PR is necessary, but it isn't sufficient. When code is being generated continuously across teams and repos, leaders need to see across teams and repos. The same way they see across any other part of their operation. This is the first release in that direction. More coming. We care about the software health as a whole, not only PR-by-PR, and there is a lot of innovation needed here. Read more: https://t.co/S6aqusTIg6

I highly recommend this. The Agentic Review is a new podcast from @QodoAI hosted by Itamar Friedman and Nnenna Ndukwe, and it's a great AI coding show that's neither hype nor doom. It's honest conversations about what shipping high-quality AI-generated code actually looks like. We need more of these conversations around AI agents. As an AI engineer, I think about this stuff constantly. A few things make this show worth your time: * It's a conversation about what good code means in the era of coding agents. * The hosts actually push back at guests instead of doing softball interviews. * The current guest lineup is strong: @dexhorthy, @shanselman, and @fullstackpython. I work a lot on context engineering, so the Dexter Horthy episode resonates the most for me. His take on context engineering as one of the biggest moats right now matches exactly what I'm seeing in production. He talks about a five-month experiment where his team stopped reading the code, then ripped it all out and rebuilt it by hand. That lesson about owning your context and actually reading what your agents produce is something every AI engineer needs to understand today. The bigger thesis across episodes: typing code may be dying, but the SDLC, code review, and craft matter more than ever. AI sprinkled on a broken software lifecycle is "a band-aid on cancer" (Hanselman's line, and it stuck with me). Thanks, @QodoAI, for the partnership on this post.

AI is making it easier than ever to build software. That does not mean it is making it easier to build good software. I sat down with @nnennahacks, Lead of Developer Relations at @QodoAI, for a conversation about AI engineering, code quality, DevRel, content creation, and what developers need to be paying attention to right now. One of the biggest themes we kept coming back to: AI can help you move faster, but speed without engineering judgment creates a new kind of mess. You can ship more code. You can generate more features. You can prototype things that used to take weeks in a matter of hours. But if no one is reading the code, testing it, understanding the architecture, or thinking about what happens six months from now, you are not accelerating engineering. You are accelerating technical debt. Nnenna made a great point in this conversation: the companies that earn developer trust are not the ones screaming “AI will replace you.” They are the ones building tools and workflows around augmentation, quality, and better developer experience. We also talked about: - Why hand-coding still matters - How AI changes the learning path for new developers - Why DevRel is becoming a much broader skillset - How content creation helps developers stand out - Why public speaking is really about vulnerability and trust - How to build a personal manifesto so your content actually sounds like you This was one of those conversations that started with AI tools and ended up being about craft, taste, trust, and doing the hard things. Episode is live now. Link in comments...

Listen to Cat. This interview with @lennysan captures a shift we’re all feeling: what it means to be a PM (and build products) in the age of AI. My takeaways (mostly her words, with some tweaks for how we think about this at @QodoAI): • Product vision = what the product needs to be in 3–6 months. PMs = what we need to do today to get there. • It’s the product role to make sure engineering can ship as smoothly as possible, not to be the bottleneck. • Speed mindset: Eng or Product has an idea → how do we get it to users by the end of the week? Not always realistic, but a useful forcing function. • Ship early, explicitly: most features go out as “research preview.” Sets expectations and accelerates learning. We should lean more into clear alpha/beta labeling. • Dogfooding → handoff: engineers share updates, marketing picks it up. Product doesn’t have to sit in every loop. • Prioritization is the PM job. Comes from deep market understanding + judgment. Also means being okay letting non-core things go. • 30% of time using our own product to discover what it can’t do yet. • Stay chill. The AI market is chaotic, that’s part of the game. • Empowered engineering: engineers making product decisions themselves. This only works if: everyone deeply understands the vision (our responsibility) engineers have strong product mindset (hiring + culture) • (Provocative) PMs, and even designers, shipping code. Not always applicable, but directionally interesting. Not saying we should copy everything, but this was a great thought teaser / validator for how fast teams can operate. As a CEO, I also see myself as part of the PMs, and I ship code.

Live on @QuantConnect for the last month, running three variations of a pretty simple HTF swing/mean reversion strategy. Started with $30k in each of them. Med vol strat - 23% over 37 days, 4.01 Sharpe High vol strat - 23.5% over 30 days, 2.99 Sharpe (shows lower return as return resets when you restart the algo) Low vol + liquidation strat - 20.6% over 30 days, unsure on Sharpe Capacity is $2-5m, so lots of room to grow. Exciting to be live and profitable! Lessons: Algo trading suits my life (45 with wife and 3 kids and coaching kid's sports teams) much better than discretionary. Because I've modeled and expect drawdowns, I welcome it rather than stressing about it, as I know that I can't make profit with risk. I get a lot of confidence from backtests, but until you go live you never really know if it's going to work. I optimise for the quarterly liquidation wicks on things like Trump tariffs, FTX, etc, as the only thing that really matters for crypto is how correlated they are during times of high stress! Overall - love algo trading, and find majors waaaay easier than the Solana memecoin system I have, as I don't need to be on the watch for a continuous stream of rugs.

🔥@ReadyPlayerMe has been acquired by Netflix. The company started over a decade ago as a small avatar experiment by young builders from Estonia. What began as a niche idea around digital identity has grown into a widely used avatar platform across games and virtual worlds. As part of the acquisition, the Ready Player Me team will join Netflix’s gaming division to work on cross game avatar technology. The focus is on enabling players to carry their identity and fandom seamlessly across different games and experiences. The journey involved years of experimentation, failed ideas, rapid learning, and close collaboration with thousands of developers. That process ultimately shaped Ready Player Me into a scalable product with global adoption. The acquisition aligns with a long held vision of open avatars that are not locked into a single game or platform. With Netflix’s scale and reach, that vision now has the opportunity to reach a much broader audience. This move marks another step in Netflix’s expansion into gaming and interactive experiences.

Show me your calendar, I’ll show you why you’re stuck. Founders hit a wall when they try to scale with old responsibilities shackled to their feet. This weekend, I did a 1-hr audit using ChatGPT + @reclaimai to map how I actually spend time vs. how I should. Here’s what I found: 1.I wear all the hats: founder, C-level, mid-mgmt, hands-on. The first two cannot be delegated, but 80% of the rest can and should be. 2.I spend lots of time on thought leadership & hiring. Formally, they’re not “mandatory,” but it reflects my values and moves the needle. 3.I actively make space to learn: from advisors, partners, and reading. This is critical for balancing short and long term. 4.The more I invest in mid-management, the more they want to step up, and the more I can. That’s the best time investment long term. Highly recommend this exercise. 1 hour can unlock your next level.

When @Replit said I could publish an app on the Apple app store, I was up for the adventure. Trust, but verify. 🤭 Submitted late last Friday night. Approved and in the app store 48 hours later. 🤯 and also 🥳 Core Values: Find Yours https://t.co/PY7RuBLHnV WHY CORE VALUES? Knowing your core values will help you understand why you make certain choices, recognize when something feels off, and live with more intention. When something is in conflict with one of your core values, it leads to internal conflict and relationship friction. Knowing your core values and the core values of others will help you minimize conflict because you learn their behaviors and decisions align with their core values, not yours. For example, if one of your top 5 core values is family, and your spouse doesn't have family anywhere on their list, it will help you both understand why you want to spend time with your extended family, but your spouse does not. It's simply not something they value. From this understanding, you can compromise - maybe you spend more time with family while your spouse joins for 1 out of 3 family events. There are dozens of examples. I love that it helps you quickly get to the root cause of conflict so resentment and feeling, "misunderstood,” happen less frequently. ——— And yes, I know there are other core values apps. And yes, I know mine is easier to use. 👩🏻‍💻 And yes, I’m charging $2.99 one-time for the app because I wanted to see how easy the integration with @RevenueCat would be. (so smooth, btw) Thanks to Replit (Canvas) for also making the video and to the Replit Community team for keeping us in the loop on all the new features! ⬇️ @MannyBernabe @raymmar @Franciscocrz

For years, I had ideas in my head that I couldn’t actually build. In my @Replit Edu spotlight, I shared how I went from adapting lessons by hand as a teacher to building the tools I kept wishing existed for my students. Teachers are already building every day. We design around student needs, modify materials, use multiple modalities, troubleshoot and create solutions with whatever we have. But now, AI literacy has changed what's possible for me and my students. It gave me a way to build from inside the classroom experience, not outside of it. And I think that’s where the future of EdTech gets really exciting: when the people closest to the problem can build the solution. A special thank you to the Replit Edu team for having me on today. Here's how I built a voice agent to do discovery calls. Her name is Annie. She is our assistant. She has a phone number on the @retellai platform which is backed by @twilio. Annie picks up the phone, talks to a prospect for about 15 minutes, and a fully written-up transcript is the final output. This is what's happening under the hood, in six steps. 1. The call comes in Annie picks up in about a second. Her voice is ElevenLabs Cimo, which sounds like a real person who's glad you called, not a robot reading a script. Her brain is GPT-5.2. I used 5.2 as the model that got the job done. A good balance between intelligence and cost. 2. She checks if you should even be on the call Before she says hi, the system looks up your phone number to make sure you actually booked an assessment. → No assessment on file ... she politely says goodbye → Assessment on file ... she uses one credit and moves on. This keeps our costs manageable. 3. She introduces herself Thirty seconds, tops. She says her name, tells you the call will take about 15 minutes, confirms who you are and what you do, and asks if you're ready to start. Sounds small. It's not. Setting the frame upfront is the difference between a focused call and a rambling one. 4. She listens. Wide angle. Five minutes of broad discovery. What's your business. What's your role. What tools do you use every day. Where does the friction live. She's told to listen, not diagnose. Most humans jump to solutions in minute two. Annie doesn't. She just learns your world and quietly clocks your top pain points. 5. She goes deep on the two biggest pain points Now she zeroes in. Five to seven minutes on the top two things she heard. How many hours a week does this cost you? What would "better" actually look like? She picks two and goes hard, instead of skimming ten. This is where the call earns its keep. By the end, we know exactly where AI would move the needle and roughly what it's worth in time. 6. She wraps up and writes the transcript analysis Annie gives you a plain-English recap of what she heard. Sets clear next steps. Ends the call. Then GPT-5.2 takes the transcript and pulls it apart into structured data: → Top pain points → Hours wasted per week → Tools in play → Sentiment Analysis The data is sent via webhook where we kick off an AI Tools Assessment skill that we custom wrote. That's how we tactically use AI. You don't need to build a rocket ship... Just know how to connect a couple pieces together and solve a pain point or two...

Someone paid $4,200 on Day 1 of @rocketdotnew 1.0. Annual plan. Solve + Build + Intelligence. No sales call. No discount. No free trial expiring. They watched the video, read what we shipped, and decided it was worth a year's commitment before the launch day was over. I sat with that for a while. Because this is a market where every AI tool is racing to be cheaper. Where growth means discounting until the unit economics break. Where "revenue marketing" means posting ARR numbers to attract more users, you'll lose in 90 days. We went the other direction. We launched a higher-priced package. On purpose. Deep agentic capabilities cost real money to build and real money to run. We'd rather charge what the value is worth than subsidize usage and call it growth. That's a choice most companies in PLG AI space won't make. We made it on day one and someone validated it on day one. Now, ever since I wrote that gross margin post a few weeks ago, my DMs have been interesting. People asking where Rocket actually stands. And now after Rocket 1.0, DMs are on Is V1.0 a sign that V0 wasn't working. Whether this is just another wrapper looking for a story. So let me be transparent. Before Rocket 1.0 even existed, the previous version hit 8-digit annualized revenue. Real subscription money in the bank, not projections. 50%+ gross margins. 35-40% month-over-month growth. $3,000 ARPU because we focused on quality over quantity from the start. We didn't build 1.0 because something was broken. We built it because the vision demanded more than what was already working. V1.0 was six months of building while the business was running. For everyone who thinks this is a wrapper, we'll be sharing the deep engineering behind these capabilities in the coming weeks. That conversation will be worth your time. You won't see revenue marketing campaigns from us. Maybe we'll share numbers occasionally. No campaigns built around them. We run this business on fundamentals, not FOMO. It's time to bring vibe into business, instead of doing vibe business. IYKYK.

A few thoughts after seeing this announcement blow up and building the @RowsHQ AI Analyst for the past 3 years: 1. It reinforces the idea that spreadsheets aren't going anywhere. It's the only common product where any businessperson can look at and analyze data. You saw this with Claude, the Ramp labs release, Shortcut, and many others. Frontier labs are realizing this as well. 2. LLMs will go after the application layer. You can use a chat interface to ask questions, write copy, or draft an email, but the end result happens in a productivity tool somewhere else. This "somewhere else" is where collaboration happens, where (very) high margins live and where you don't need to worry about inference costs or hallucinations. 3. Spreadsheets are almost code, that's why this works. 4. Data analysis agents are world-class when they can handoff spreadsheet formulas to Python when needed. We learned that last year when we added support for Python in @RowsHQ AI Analyst. The agent automatically decides if it can solve the request using spreadsheet formulas (easier for 1B people to understand), or if it needs Python. 5. For this to ultimately replace most spreadsheet use, it needs a System 1 - System 2 type system, so that easy requests are handled very fast (routed to simpler models with less context), and hard problems take as much time as they need. That's how we architected the AI Analyst, which results in System 1 tasks taking a median of 6s (e.g. add a column with profit) and System 2 tasks taking 69s. It won't matter how smart it is at creating a DCF if it takes 30 seconds to format a range of cells. Most knowledge work is editing existing work, not creating new one. 6. The next step-function improvement is having your data analyst agent accessible everywhere you work. This means opening and analyzing CSVs from your email inbox, reading pdf receipts, answering questions on Slack, etc. 7. If you really want to see what a best-in-class spreadsheet agent can do, try https://t.co/lndfMNCbZC.

This @Scenario_gg Workflow takes any image (character, object, or scene) and automatically extracts all individual elements (typically 10–15) in one single click. It use an LLM node to identify & list each element, then SAM 3.1 from @Meta to mask them, @OpenAI GPT Image 2 + Pixa background removal to reconstruct and isolate. From there, you can group everything into a grid or keep iterating (rearranging elements, more image editing, 3D conversion, or video gen...)

Most SaaS founders ship features the wrong way. They post 1 tweet… then wonder why nobody uses it. Here’s a simple launch stack I’d use instead: 1⃣ Record a clean demo using @screenstudio or @loom 2⃣ Turn visitors into viewers with video popups using https://t.co/MOO0sk6u0F 3⃣ Send product updates with @loops or @beehiiv 4⃣ Schedule launch posts using @typefully or @taplio 5⃣ Reply to relevant Reddit/X/LinkedIn posts to distribute it manually 6⃣ Add an in-app “What’s New” banner so existing users discover it 7⃣ Reuse the same demo video into shorts, GIFs, and tweets One feature = 20+ pieces of distribution content. Most people don’t need more features. They need better distribution.

Had a great chat with Harry Kim from @nvidia , @philipkiely from @baseten , and Arya Asemanfar from @SierraPlatform, for a salon dinner on long-running agents and inference. We were joined by 20+ technical leaders from @Replit , @nvidia , @hippocraticai , @PrimeIntellect , @anyscalecompute , @togethercompute, @thinkymachines, @netflix , @tryramp , @MemGPT and more. Consistent themes that emerged through several conversations: - Nobody owns the agent harness yet. App teams are building it by default, inference platforms want to absorb it from below. Sentiment pointed to the application layer as its home — though some platforms are getting close. - Evals are the precondition for everything else — model right-sizing, routing decisions, fine-tuning confidence. Nothing off the shelf is specific or good enough to work generally. Everyone is handling it in-house. - The next wave for inference is real-time: video, audio, multimodal. Beyond that, inference-time compute and observability are the areas people are focused on. - VR as a longer-term area of interest, when the technology can serve the capabilities consumers actually need. Super energizing to chop it up with some of the best in the business! cc @felicis

Today I integrated an AI agent, called Cole, I Connected cole to my work space on @SlackHQ, asking it to debug my code which we have been on for a while now, it audited the codes done by myself and my team. TIMES HAVE CHANGED! we all know those feeling with hiring teams and assistants to manage portfolio, data, codes, communities, I think that era is over. meet: @PocodotAI's AI Agent called: COLE What is @PocodotAI all about? well, here are some interesting facts you need to know about cole or https://t.co/HDjcuqCyZn WHAT IS COLE? Cole is Pocodot's AI Chief of Staff - an orchestrating agent that breaks down complex tasks, creates plans, delegates subtasks to specialized agents, and monitors progress on your behalf. Cole also works alongside other Agents/AI, they are called: TEAMS Teams are groups of agents that work together on multi-step workflows. Each agent's output feeds into the next agent's input, creating an automated pipeline. Now Cole can be integrated with other socials or work spaces to coordinate workflows and help you catch up with past events that took place in your chats. I just tried it and its cool. Next connection would be to my @telegram and @WhatsApp to help me harness all my past activities. More importantly, remind me of things I'd miss. would keep you updated on this, also creating a video, showing the steps to connecting and working with Agent cole. #builtwithcole.

AI Agent sold ! ⚡️ Another real business workflow ready to be delegated to AI. This agent connects @github , @sentry , @linear and @SlackHQ to keep engineering teams perfectly aligned. Here’s the use case: When code is shipped and a PR is merged, the agent can monitor errors, detect spikes, create and prioritize issues in Linear, then alert the right engineering lead in Slack. No manual issue tracking. No context lost between tools. No critical bug sitting unnoticed. The agent handles the operational layer between shipping code, tracking issues and keeping the sprint updated. This is what AI agents are really about : Not replacing engineers. Removing the repetitive coordination work around them. Code shipped. Issues tracked. Sprint updated. All delegated to an AI Agent. #AI #AIAgents #Automation #DevTools #FleeceAI

Last Friday, @smallest_AI hosted a private dinner for community and GTM leaders building in AI-native communications - with friends from @stripe, @meetgranola, @AnthropicAI, @PLAUDAI, @rootlyhq, and @SentientAGI. What stood out wasn't just the energy in the room. It was how this group of forward-thinking leaders truly showed up and opened up within such a short time frame. At every dinner we host, we curate the guest list with intention - not just who we want to meet, but who each person in the room would genuinely want to sit across from, and why it makes sense for everyone at the table to be there. In one evening, we got to know each other, exchanged notes, and explored upcoming collaborations. The closers shared what actually moves large deals forward. The conference veterans shared which ones are worth attending - and the secrets to maximizing ROI. The event hosts aligned on future collaborations. Since community and GTM are, at their core, collaborative, one of the greatest forms of leverage in this work is the network you build. And the most meaningful connections come from rooms like this - dinners structured around genuine relationships as the foundation for what comes next. Grateful for everyone who made this room what it was. 🙏

SF’s community scene is something special. The welcoming atmosphere, meaningful introductions, personal stories, great conversations, and incredible food made for such a fun evening. Grateful to @smallest_AI and @lilyyang169 for bringing together such a thoughtful group of people. Looking forward to the next one

The most successful dinners aren't the "businessy" ones. They're the ones where you actually get to know the people at the table and leave wanting to see them again. Last night, @smallest_AI brought together the founder of Chatbase, founders of Cookiy AI and https://t.co/dUOn7lsAop, vertical leads from Plaud, Waymo, Capital One, and CITCON, and researchers from OpenAI and Anthropic. We talked (half of us in Mandarin) about: - Our AGI visions - Hobbies that have nothing to do with startups - Places we've traveled And when we went around the table with an icebreaker, the conversations got even richer: - How success looks different for VC-backed vs. bootstrapped founders - Writing and publishing a book - Becoming a pilates instructor - Favorite sports - The languages we speak (to humans and animals alike) These are people building at the frontier of AI. But it was the deeply human conversations that brought us together. Genuine connections are at the core of what we believe to be the best practice. Most people last night entered the room knowing only one person. Everyone left with a new group of people who are now friends. @kamath_sutra @yasser_elsaid_

If you got access to Mythos and are terrified of missing a fix, missing a vulnerable library import, missing a subsidiary, etc—shoot me a DM. @Sourcegraph gives you 100% confidence that you’re seeing and fixing the whole codebase: across all code hosts, 100s of thousands of repos, every team and division. Confidence is in rare supply these days.

See how AI software company @Sourcegraph connects its NetSuite data to Claude to surface financial anomalies faster, ask questions in natural language for real-time answers, and collaborate more effectively. https://t.co/Rlh5PJ9Tf6 https://t.co/uvbE2tSWXq

+1 to this, and not just for internal operations. We built a classifier for how our customers are using @Sourcegraph’s Deep Search agent (finding code, understanding code, incident response investigations, security, etc) and make the breakdown visible so they can learn about their own patterns and gaps.

.@Sourcegraph analysed 1,281 coding agent runs across large enterprise codebases and found the bottleneck often wasn’t the model — it was context retrieval and navigation. Agents struggled once codebases grew large enough: pulling in the wrong files, thrashing between tools, and making locally correct changes that broke systems elsewhere. One stat says a lot: a task that took one agent 96 tool calls and 84 minutes dropped to five calls and under five minutes with better retrieval infrastructure. The takeaway is this: coding agents need far better context and navigation layers around them to operate reliably in production. 🔗 Read the full story here: https://t.co/LsatesrRDC

NEW: lessons learned on how to make a REALLY great enterprise @SlackHQ bot for AI products from our experience @Sourcegraph Touches on interactive and real-time feedback (critical when agents are doing asynchronous work), summaries and previews for the Slack UI, rate limiting, and more. Thanks @juliaxu_xu__ @JasonHawkHarris & Emi for spreading the knowledge https://t.co/5zfmmv1Sk5

I was speaking to a dev tools lead at a huge (top 10) global bank about Mythos/Glasswing. Quote: “some people think we don’t need @Sourcegraph. Yeah, sure, Claude can fix this, but I have 90k repositories!”

Had a great time speaking at the @qdrant_engine x @mlopscommunity event last night. TL;DR: scientific knowledge is fragmented and weakly linked. If we surface those connections (like we do across codebases at @Sourcegraph), both humans and agents navigate better. Built a prototype on @SciXCommunity data: postgres + pgvector, semantic community clustering, NER pipelines, graph context, structural parsing -> MCP agent tooling. The ultimate goal of this work is to surface more scientific connections to reduce our collective “scientific tech debt” and accelerate discovery. Slides + demo: https://t.co/O3wxdXGiUj

this new AI can generate and edit audio faster than you think @StabilityAI just dropped Stable Audio 3 it’s a family of open-weight audio models for music and sound effects, with editing support built in the interesting part is not just generation, but variable-length output and inpainting, so you can create and modify audio more naturally 🔸the architecture is doing a lot of work here >> a compressed audio autoencoder called SAME turns stereo 44.1 kHz audio into compact latents, which makes longer generation much more efficient >> then a diffusion transformer works on those latents, conditioned on text, duration, and masks for editing 🔸what stands out most is the practical focus >> Stable Audio 3 is designed so inference cost scales with duration, instead of forcing everything through one fixed maximum length >> that makes it more usable for real workflows, especially if you care about longer clips or iterative editing 🔸stability AI also pushed the training pipeline pretty far >> they used flow matching, distillation warmup, and adversarial post-training to get fast generation without relying on classifier-free guidance at inference >> that means fewer steps, less overhead, and a more efficient generation loop 🔸the lineup is pretty clear too: small, medium, and large models >> small and medium weights are available openly, while large is enterprise-only >> for most people, the main takeaway is simple: this is a serious step forward for audio generation and editing, not just another demo

Experiment: Painting sound effects with @StabilityAI Stable Audio 3 1. Free-form drawing on a spectrogram-like canvas. Time on the x-axis, pitch on the y. 2. Synthesise the drawing to audio. Strokes control bandpass-filtering of a white noise source. 3. Use that audio as input for SA3 audio-to-audio pass combined with a text prompt. In Claude Desktop with a handful of MCP tools.

AI 산업은 이제 더 이상 “데이터 문제는 애매하다”는 식으로 넘어갈 수 없는 지점에 도달하고 있는 것 같습니다. 지금 창작 산업 전반에서 벌어지는 일들을 보면 흐름이 너무 명확합니다. 비주얼 아티스트들은 @StabilityAI와 @midjourney를 상대로 스크래핑된 작품 문제로 소송을 진행하고 있고, 음악 업계는 @suno와 @udiomusic이 대규모로 저작권 음악을 학습했다며 대응하고 있습니다. 이제는 3D 아티스트들까지 @Meta, @nvidia, @Roblox를 상대로 자사(proprietary) 3D 자산 학습 문제로 소송을 제기하고 있습니다. 심지어 @Disney와 @UniversalPics 같은 거대한 엔터테인먼트 기업들도 AI 시스템이 보호된 캐릭터와 상업 IP를 지나치게 정확하게 재현하기 시작하자 직접 움직이고 있습니다. 흥미로운 것은 거의 모든 소송이 결국 같은 문제를 가리키고 있다는 점입니다. 인터넷에는 AI 시대에 필요한 “소유권 인프라”가 존재하지 않았다는 사실입니다. 오랫동안 업계는 아주 단순한 방식으로 움직였습니다. 먼저 스크래핑하고, 빠르게 확장하고, 논쟁은 나중에 처리하는 구조였습니다. AI가 아직 실험적인 단계일 때는 그 방식이 통했습니다. 하지만 이제 AI 생성 콘텐츠가 실제 비즈니스와 산업 안으로 들어오기 시작하면서 모두가 같은 현실을 깨닫고 있습니다. 결국 이 산업을 움직인 연료는 인간의 창작물이었습니다. 최근 칸 영화제에서 Demi Moore가 했던 말이 이 상황을 정말 잘 설명한다고 느꼈습니다. “반대는 또 다른 반대를 만든다. AI는 이미 존재한다. 그렇기 때문에 AI와 싸우는 것은 결국 우리가 질 싸움을 하는 것이다. 중요한 것은 우리가 AI와 어떻게 함께 갈 수 있을지를 찾는 것이다.” 많은 사람들이 아직 놓치고 있는 부분이 바로 이것 같습니다. 미래는 “AI vs 아티스트”가 아닙니다. AI는 이미 존재합니다. 진짜 중요한 문제는 창작자들이 그 가치 흐름 안에 포함될 것인가, 아니면 조용히 배제될 것인가입니다. 그래서 지금 @campnetworkxyz는 단순한 프로젝트라기보다, 꼭 필요한 인프라처럼 느껴집니다. Camp가 Origin과 mAItrix를 통해 만들고 있는 구조는 업계가 계속 해결하지 못했던 문제를 정면으로 다루고 있습니다. 모델이 이미 학습된 뒤에 몇 년 동안 소송을 진행하는 대신, 처음부터 IP를 등록하고, 출처를 고정하며, 라이선스 권한을 정의하고, AI 시스템이나 에이전트가 그 데이터를 합법적으로 학습하면서 사용량에 따라 투명하게 보상이 이루어지도록 만드는 구조입니다. 이것은 AI와 창작자의 관계 자체를 바꾸는 일입니다. 아티스트는 더 이상 자신의 작업이 어딘가 데이터셋에 몰래 흡수되었는지 불안해하지 않아도 됩니다. 음악가는 단지 사용 여부를 증명하기 위해 수년간 소송을 이어갈 필요가 없습니다. 작가 역시 플랫폼이 “윤리적으로 행동해주길” 기대하는 데 의존하지 않아도 됩니다. 시스템 자체가 출처, 권한, 보상을 처리하게 되는 것입니다. 그리고 솔직히 말해서, 저는 업계가 결국 이 방향으로 갈 수밖에 없다고 생각합니다. 이미 기업들도 무분별한 스크래핑에서 라이선스 기반 구조로 이동하기 시작했습니다. 법적으로도, 상업적으로도 그렇지 않으면 확장이 불가능해지고 있기 때문입니다. 이제 이 소송들은 단순한 법적 충돌이 아니라, 기존 인터넷 구조가 AI 시대의 압력을 버티지 못하고 있다는 신호처럼 보입니다. 얼마 전 한 창작자 친구가 이런 말을 했습니다. AI 자체보다 더 힘든 것은 “불확실함”이라고요. 자신의 작업이 어디로 가는지, 누가 학습하는지, 그리고 그로 인해 생긴 가치와 자신이 연결될 수 있는지 전혀 알 수 없다는 점이 사람들을 점점 지치게 만든다고 했습니다. 그래서 @campnetworkxyz 같은 인프라가 중요해집니다. 불확실함을 규칙과 출처, 그리고 프로그래머블한 소유권 구조로 바꾸기 때문입니다. 개인적으로 저는 앞으로 AI 시대의 승자는 단순히 가장 큰 모델을 가진 기업이 아닐 거라고 생각합니다. 결국 창작자들이 신뢰할 수 있는 생태계를 만든 쪽이 살아남게 될 가능성이 훨씬 크다고 봅니다.

Something just dropped that most builders haven't noticed yet. @subquadratic shipped the world's first commercial subquadratic LLM. 12 million token context window. nearly 1,000× less compute than transformers. ~1/20th the cost of Claude Opus on long-context tasks. For 8 years, every major AI system, GPT, Claude, and Gemini has been built on the same transformer math from 2017. SubQ just broke that ceiling. And most people are still arguing about which chatbot is smarter. and most people are still arguing about which chatbot is smarter. #ai #subq #llm #buildinpublic #aiagents

@AppenResearch independently evaluated @subquadratic's SSA kernel - a learned sparse attention mechanism designed to reduce the quadratic scaling limitations of full attention. Results at 1M-token context lengths: - 56.2× wall clock speedup vs. FA2 - 62.8× FLOP reduction (validated via torch.profiler, <4% variance from theoretical) - 95.6% average score across RULER tasks at 128K - 86.2% average score on the hardest MRCR 8-needle bucket (512K–1M contexts) - 81.8% SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate Full report: https://t.co/9OpvaqDPbt

The cost to run GPT-3.5 level intelligence fell 280x in two years. On PhD-level reasoning tasks, inference costs are dropping 40x per year and now @subquadratic’ 52x cheaper long-context on top of that. Every major platform shift followed a compute cost collapse. + Cheap bandwidth gave us streaming + Cheap mobile compute gave us Instagram AI inference is collapsing faster than either of those curves. The apps that win next are the ones that use the most intelligence per dollar, not the least.

AI’s biggest bottleneck was never intelligence. It was memory. Every AI company built an entire infrastructure stack around one limitation: LLMs cannot actually remember very much at once. 🤯 That is how AI memory became a workaround economy. @subquadratic's bet is that the fix is not better duct tape. It is an architecture that can actually read millions of tokens at once. Today, the startup launched SubQ: ⚡ A 12M-token LLM ⚡ 1/5 the cost of frontier models ⚡ 52× faster than FlashAttention at 1M tokens ⚡ Better long-context retrieval scores than GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Claude Opus 4.7 in MRCR v2

Who > What. @suno just announced a well-deserved funding round at a $5.4B valuation. I was fortunate to be their sole pre-seed investor, but I have to confess, I didn’t love the music business when we invested back in 2022. At the time, crypto was king and AI was still a novelty. Music apps are a notoriously challenging category. ChatGPT hadn’t yet landed in the hands of users and showed what was possible. But this team marched to the beat of their own drum. When I met @MikeyShulman, Martin and Georg, they were a trio of wizards who understood audio and AI better than anyone on the planet. I had misgivings about the music business, but had total conviction about the team. Suno wasn’t actively fundraising, so I pitched @fcollective. Luckily Mikey thought we may be able to add some value ... It was well worth the effort. Markets evolve, products pivot, and technologies come in and out of fashion, but brilliant, authentic, agentic founders are the constant around which great startups are built. Invest in the "who,” trust that the “what” will follow.

Grammarly paid ~$825M for Superhuman last July. The whole brand is sub-100ms speed. LLMs typically don't run in 100ms... So how do you build AI into a product where slowness breaks the magic? Loïc Houssier, VP of Engineering at @Superhuman, came on @chain_ofthought and walked through it: pre-caching voice context so only the dictation hits the model, starting every feature on a frontier model like Opus then fine-tuning down to cheap BERT classifiers, and classifying "makes the user look foolish" as a P0 bug. Same severity as a crash. Here's how they built AI into Superhuman with zero latency 👇 Chapters: 00:00 Cold open: pattern detection beats new tools 00:18 Loïc's path 02:13 Retrofitting AI into a 100ms product 04:08 Voice on mobile: pre-caching LLM context 07:46 Frontier first, then fine-tune 11:04 The "double-dipping" trick that worked on GPT-4 and stopped 12:25 Cognitive load 16:59 YC urgency vs peer CTO grounding 19:28 Pods, AI Guild, aligned autonomy 23:15 Managing models vs managing people 28:27 The Waymo example: eigenvectors of evaluation 32:15 Day 30: leading the AI Inbox sprint 35:04 Why email is the killer agent use case 38:51 Auto-draft, never auto-send 39:57 Agent agency vs laziness 43:07 Hiring for AI fluency 45:55 Pattern detection is the leadership skill 47:21 Nuclear submarines 48:37 Closing 49:38 Superhuman is hiring
